data.title: Chinese sellers on Amazon mull price hikes or US exit as Trump slaps 125% tariff
data.description:
Chinese companies that sell products on Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) are reportedly preparing to hike prices for the U.S. or quit the market due to the "unprecedented blow" from President Donald Trump's tariff hikes, the head of China's largest e-commerce association said.

"This isn't just a tax issue, it's that the entire cost structure gets entirely overwhelmed," said Wang Xin, the head of the Shenzhen Cross-Border E-Commerce Association, which represents more than 3,000 Amazon sellers told Reuters.

"It'll be very hard for anyone to survive in the U.S. market," she said, noting the tariffs could also lead to customs delays and higher logistics costs.

Some sellers are also looking to increase prices in the U.S. while others are looking to find new markets, the report added.

On Wednesday, U.S. President Donald Trump announced he would raise the tariff on Chinese imports from 125% to 104%.

Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) has meanwhile cancelled orders that originate from China and Southeast Asian countries, leaving certain vendors on the hook with unsold inventory.

Related tickers: (NASDAQ:MCHI), (NYSEARCA:FXI), (NYSEARCA:GXC), (NYSEARCA:KWEB), (NYSEARCA:CQQQ), (FLCH), (CNYA), (YINN), (ASHR)

data.title: Japan producer prices rise more than expected to 4.2% in March
data.description:
Producer prices in Japan increased by 4.2% y/y in March 2025, surpassing market estimates of 3.9% and following a marginally revised 4.1% growth in the previous month, marking the 49th straight month of producer inflation, with cost rising further for most components.

Monthly, producer prices grew by 0.4%, quickening from an upwardly revised 0.2% gain in January and marking the steepest rise in three months.

Separate data showed, the value of loans in Japan increased 2.8% year-on-year in March 2025, slowing from a 3.1% gain in February which was the highest reading in seven months. 

The Nikkei 225 Index surged 8% to above 34,000 on Thursday, erasing losses from earlier this week, after US President Donald Trump unexpectedly lowered new tariff rates on imports from most US trade partners to 10% for 90 days to allow for trade negotiations. The Japanese yen appreciated past 147 per U.S. dollar on Thursday, continuing to trade within a volatile range as markets responded to shifting global trade dynamics.

More on Japan

  • Japan’s unemployment rate unexpectedly drops to 2.4% in February
  • Japan's Feb industrial production exceeds expectations, retail sales fall short of forecasts
  • Japan’s manufacturing downturn extends to ninth month; services activity turns negative
  • Japan’s core inflation rate slows to 3% in February, exceeds forecast; food prices slow slightly
  • Japan’s trade balance swung to surplus in February, driven by strong exports

data.title: ChatGPT maker OpenAI countersues Elon Musk for relentless harassment
data.description:
OpenAI has countersued billionaire Elon Musk, alleging harassment and his attempts to sabotage the company's transition to a for-profit and asking a federal judge to stop Musk from any "further unlawful and unfair action."

"Through press attacks, malicious campaigns broadcast to Musk's more than 200 million followers on the social media platform he controls, a pretextual demand for corporate records, harassing legal claims, and a sham bid for OpenAI's assets, Musk has tried every tool available to harm OpenAI," the company said in a filing in Musk's existing lawsuit against OpenAI.

Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT)-backed OpenAI's countersuit is in response to Musk's earlier lawsuit that accused the ChatGPT maker of abandoning its original mission to develop AI for humanity’s benefit. However, a transition to a for-profit model is crucial for the company to secure $40 billion in funding.

"Elon's nonstop actions against us are just bad-faith tactics to slow down OpenAI and seize control of the leading AI innovations for his personal benefit." OpenAI said in a post on social media platform X.

Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) owner Elon Musk co-founded OpenAI in 2015 but left in 2018 and has criticized the organization's shift toward commercialization, arguing it diverges from its original mission to develop AI for societal benefit. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman rejected Musk's takeover bid earlier this year, emphasizing that the company is not for sale.

To prevent OpenAI from becoming a for-profit company, Musk requested a preliminary injunction; however, the federal court rejected Elon's request in March but allowed the case to go to a jury trial in the spring of 2026.

“OpenAI is resilient,” reads the countersuit filing. “But Musk’s actions have taken a toll. Should his campaign persist, greater harm is threatened — to OpenAI’s ability to govern in service of its mission, to the relationships that are essential to furthering that mission, and to the public interest.”

data.title: China's March inflation drops for second straight month; issues warning on travel to U.S.
data.description:
China's consumer prices fell by 0.1% year-on-year in March 2025, missing market expectations of a 0.1% increase and marking the second consecutive month of drop, as the ongoing trade dispute with the U.S. threatens to exert further downward pressure on prices. China also issued a US travel warning, citing economic and security issues.

China’s producer prices dropped by 2.5% y/y in March 2025, steeper than market expectations of a 2.3% drop and February’s 2.2% decrease, marking the fastest annual fall since last November.

Core inflation, which excludes volatile food and fuel prices, rose 0.5% in March, rebounding from a 0.1% decrease in February.

On a monthly basis, the CPI declined by 0.4%, a steeper fall than a 0.2% drop in February, marking the second straight month of contraction.

On a monthly basis, producer prices shrank 0.4%, the largest drop in six months, after a 0.1% dip in February. During Q1 of 2025, factory-gate prices fell by 2.3%, following a 2.2% decline in 2024.

China's food prices fell 1.4% year-over-year in March 2025, notably easing from a 3.3% drop in the previous month and marking the second straight month of decline.

While US President Trump announced a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for non-retaliating countries, he increased tariffs on Chinese imports to 125%, following Beijing’s announcement of an 84% retaliatory levy on American goods.

Separately, amid rising tensions and new tariffs (including 125% on China), China advises against US travel due to economic and security issues. The advisory from the Chinese Ministry of Culture and Tourism came as both nations imposed fresh retaliatory tariffs. The U.S. currently has China on a level two travel advisory, a downgrade from level three (“reconsider travel”) that followed a prisoner swap last November.

The Shanghai Composite rose 0.6% to climb above 3,200, while the Shenzhen Component jumped 3.2% to 9,840 on Thursday, marking the third consecutive session of gains for mainland equities even as trade tensions with the U.S. intensified, and the offshore yuan depreciated to around 7.36 per dollar, pressured by deepening deflationary concerns.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:FXI), (NYSEARCA:KWEB), (NYSEARCA:CQQQ), (NASDAQ:MCHI), (NYSEARCA:ASHR), (NYSEARCA:YINN), (NYSE:TDF), (NYSEARCA:CHIQ), (NYSEARCA:GXC), (NYSEARCA:EWH), (NYSEARCA:KBA), (NYSEARCA:YANG), (NASDAQ:CXSE), (NYSE:CAF), (NYSEARCA:CWEB), (NASDAQ:PGJ), (NYSEARCA:KURE).

data.title: PTL Limited prices $7M share offering
data.description:
PTL Limited (NASDAQ:PTLE) priced its public offering of 23.8M ordinary shares at a public offering price of $0.30 per ordinary share.

Gross proceeds are expected to be approximately $7.14M.

The offering is expected to close on April 10, 2025.

The stock price traded 0.77% higher on Wednesday during after market hours of trading.

data.title: Thursday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
8:30 AM CPI

The CPI is a measure of the change in the average price level of a fixed basket of goods and services purchased by consumers. Monthly changes in the CPI represent the rate of inflation for the consumer.

The consensus sees CPI for March up a modest 0.1 percent on the month and 2.6 percent on year, after 0.2 percent and 2.8 percent in February.

8:30 AM Jobless Claims

New unemployment claims are compiled weekly to show the number of individuals who filed for unemployment insurance for the first time. Claims are seen up to 225K after falling unexpectedly by 6K to 219K in the previous week.

8:30 AM Fed's Barkin Speech

Richmond Federal Reserve Bank President Thomas Barkin will speak on"Driving Through the Economic Fog" and participate in a moderated question-and-answer session before the Talbot County Business Appreciation Summit sponsored by the Talbot County Chamber of Commerce.

9:30 AM Fed's Logan Speech

Dallas Federal Reserve Bank President Lorie Logan will give welcome remarks before hybrid Outlook for North American Trade and Immigration event hosted by the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas.

10:00 AM Fed's Schmid Speech

Kansas City Federal Reserve Bank President Jeffrey Schmid will speak on the economic outlook and monetary policy before the Secured Finance Network Independent Finance Roundtable 2025.

10:00 AM Fed's Bowman Speech

Federal Reserve Board Governor Michelle Bowman Senate Banking Committee will hold a hearing on the nomination of Michelle Bowman to Federal Reserve vice chair for supervision.

10:30 AM EIA Natural Gas Report

The Energy Information Administration (EIA) provides weekly information on natural gas stocks in underground storage for the U.S. and five regions of the country. The level of inventories helps determine prices for natural gas products.

12:00 PM Fed's Goolsbee Speech

Chicago Federal Reserve Bank President Austan Goolsbee participates in a moderated question-and-answer session before the Economic Club of New York.

12:00 PM Fed's Harker Speech

Philadelphia Federal Reserve Bank President Patrick Harker speaks on"Fintech" before the 2025 Fintech and Financial Institutions Research Conference.

1:00 PM 30-Yr Bond Auction

The US Treasury Department will hold an auction for $22 billion of 30-year auction.

2:00 PM Treasury Statement

The U.S. Treasury releases a monthly account of the surplus or deficit of the federal government. Changes in the budget balance reflect Federal policy on spending and taxation. The government's fiscal year begins in October. The deficit is expected at $163.0 billion in March, versus a deficit of $237 billion in the year ago month.

4:30 PM Fed Balance Sheet

The Fed balance sheet is a weekly report listing the assets and liabilities of the Federal Reserve System.

data.title: Verizon's AI tools using Google tech deliver better customer service, boost sales
data.description:
Google's products (NASDAQ:GOOGL) (NASDAQ:GOOG) have improved the quality of customer service queries by 95% and have helped increase sales for Verizon.

The telecom's AI-powered customer service solution utilizes Google Cloud's generative AI technology, including the Vertex AI platform, Gemini models, and Customer Engagement Suite, the companies said Wednesday.

The CEO of Verizon's consumer group business, Sampath Sowmyanarayan, said, "Our collaboration with Google Cloud and the integration of Gemini into our customer care platforms mark a significant advancement in our commitment to providing exceptional customer experiences."

Verizon's "Personal Research Assistant," deployed across 28,000 customer care reps and retail stores, has helped frontline workers with real-time, context-aware, and personalized answers to questions from customers, the companies said.

Earlier in the day, Sowmyanarayan told Reuters that sales are up nearly 40% at Verizon since deploying the AI tool.

Verizon's solution was created after feeding 15,000 internal documents into a flagship version of Gemini LLM. "We are doing reskilling in real time, from customer care agents to selling agents," he said.

data.title: Canadian Solar, Fluence Energy among clean techs seen as potential take-private targets
data.description:
Canadian Solar (NASDAQ:CSIQ) closed +21% and Fluence Energy (NASDAQ:FLNC) +13.3% in Wednesday's trading, as Minotaur Capital co-founder Armina Rosenberg tabbed the two renewable energy companies as potential future take-private targets.

Canadian Solar (NASDAQ:CSIQ) and Fluence (NASDAQ:FLNC), whose shares have plunged 26% and 72% YTD even including Wednesday's gains, could "benefit from a take-private to allow for restructuring to realign operations and invest in domestic capacity," Rosenberg told Bloomberg.

There could also be opportunities in firms involved in energy management software, grid optimization or smart energy systems "that have solid underlying tech, but face market volatility," she added.

Private funds and lenders see some publicly-traded renewable energy and clean technology firms as prime targets for acquisition and de-listing, according to Bloomberg, as valuations have been under pressure from President Trump's focus on fossil fuels, and now the global trade war is expected to strain their international supply chains.

Alternative energy names shot higher Wednesday, along with most of the stock market, after President Trump announced a 90-day pause and lowering of tariffs, except for levies on China.

Examples include SolarEdge Technologies (SEDG) +15.8%, Shoals Technologies (SHLS) +15.4%, GE Vernova (GEV) +13.9%, Array Technologies (ARRY) +13.8%, Sunrun (RUN) +13.5%, Ballard Power Systems (BLDP) +12.7%, Enphase Energy (ENPH) +9.3%, JinkoSolar (JKS) +8.9%, Plug Power (PLUG) +8%, Sunnova Energy (NOVA) +7.9%, First Solar (FSLR) +7.7%.

ETFs: (TAN), (ICLN), (QCLN), (PBW), (PBD), (ACES), (CNRG), (ERTH), (SMOG)

data.title: Trump signs order to boost U.S. shipbuilding, taps Musk’s team for efficiency overhaul
data.description:
In a move aimed at reviving the U.S. shipbuilding industry and countering China’s growing dominance at sea, President Donald Trump signed an executive order Wednesday that outlines a sweeping plan to enhance domestic maritime capabilities.

The order includes new tax incentives and infrastructure investment opportunities, particularly in areas designated as “maritime opportunity zones.” It also tasks government agencies with reexamining the United States' naval presence in strategically important regions such as the Arctic.

A key element of the initiative involves deploying a specialized task force from the Department of Government Efficiency — led by Elon Musk — to assess inefficiencies in the Navy’s shipbuilding process. The team will look at high-cost and delayed programs, including the troubled $22 billion frigate initiative, with an eye toward streamlining production and delivery.

Countering Chinese dominance

The executive order comes as the U.S. faces increasing pressure from China’s rapidly expanding shipbuilding sector. In the past 25 years, China has grown from producing a sliver of the world’s commercial ships to controlling more than half of the global market.

"Zero percent of the containers used to move commodities around the world are built by the United States, compared to 96% built by China," the administration said. 

China’s naval fleet has also surpassed that of the U.S. in sheer size, according to the White House.

To combat this, the order outlines measures to support investment in the domestic maritime sector, including tariffs and fees on Chinese-built vessels entering American ports. Revenue from these tariffs would be funneled into a fund supporting U.S. shipbuilders and suppliers.

Additional fees will apply to cargo that arrives through Canadian or Mexican ports before entering the U.S., closing a loophole that labor unions have long criticized.

Auditing shipbuilding plans

In addition to Musk’s review, the Navy and Coast Guard are instructed to launch a 45-day audit of current and future shipbuilding plans. This review will go beyond prior evaluations and is expected to offer recommendations on how to speed up the delivery of delayed vessels, such as Virginia-class submarines and the next-generation ballistic missile sub.

The order also reiterates a renewed focus on Arctic operations. Trump previously called for a fleet of domestically produced icebreaker ships to bolster U.S. influence in the increasingly contested region.

Although a proposal from the U.S. Trade Representative to impose million-dollar port entry fees on Chinese ships is still under consideration, the new order — titled "Restoring America’s Maritime Dominance" — provides a roadmap for how the U.S. plans to turn trade policy into industrial strategy.

data.title: Elon Musk adds $36B to wealth in single day as markets soar on tariff pause
data.description:
Elon Musk was the biggest winner in a historic windfall for the world’s richest people on Wednesday, as global stock markets surged and the Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) chief executive added a staggering $36 billion to his net worth in just one day, Bloomberg News reported.

The massive gain came as President Donald Trump announced a pause on tariffs targeting some U.S. trading partners, triggering a sharp rebound in the markets. The S&P 500 Index (SP500) recorded its biggest jump since 2008, while the Nasdaq Composite posted its strongest single-day gain in more than 24 years.

Musk's fortune soared alongside Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) shares, which rallied 23% on the news, making him the top individual gainer of the day by a wide margin. The spike marks one of the largest single-day wealth increases ever recorded for a billionaire, further cementing Musk’s place at the top of the Bloomberg Billionaires Index.

The day proved historic across the board. The world’s 500 richest people added a combined $304 billion to their net worths — the largest one-day gain since Bloomberg launched its index in 2013. On average, each billionaire saw a 3.5% jump in personal wealth.

Following Musk, Meta (NASDAQ:META) Chief Executive Mark Zuckerberg gained nearly $26 billion, while Nvidia’s Jensen Huang saw a $15.5 billion boost as chipmaker stocks rebounded. Carvana (NYSE:CVNA) Chief Executive Ernest Garcia III notched the day’s largest percentage gain, with his wealth increasing by 25% — up $1.4 billion, according to Bloomberg News.

Wednesday’s rally reversed some of the sharp losses from the previous week, when a sweeping tariff announcement from the Trump administration triggered a market sell-off and wiped out billions in wealth. But with tensions easing, investors raced back into equities, powering one of the most profitable days ever for the world’s elite — led, as usual, by Elon Musk.
